coredata.dc:title: Guiding charge transfer kinetics into cocatalyst for efficient solar water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lin
Xiong, Yuli
Xiao, Peng
Zhang, Yunhuai
coredata.dc:description: The combination of transition metal-based cocatalysts with semiconductors is a promising route to improve the photoelectrochemical performance in solar water splitting. However, it is difficult to evaluate the catalytic ability for cocatalysts quantitatively. Herein, we use kelvin probe force microscopy technique, combining with Mott-Schottky method, to investigate the cocatalysts from the point of surface potential and Femi level. Through synthesis the nickel/cobalt oxide nanoparticles and nanoporous bismuth vanadate (BiVO4), the result indicates the surface potential of NiCoO2 coated BiVO4 is lower than pristine BiVO4 and BiVO4 coated by NiO and CoO cocatalysts respectively. The lower surface potential means a more upward band bending structure at the interface of electrode and electrolyte, which can promote the separation of electron-hole pairs and enhance the charge transfer kinetics. Thus, the NiCoO2/BiVO4 photoanode achieve a remarkable photocurrent density of 2.34 mA/cm2 for water oxidation, and the charge separation and oxidation kinetics efficiencies are 61.9% and 72.7% respectively. In all, our studies provide a new mechanistic insight into cocatalysts for solar energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
Surface potential
Fermi level
Cocatalysts
Photoelectrochemical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of OEC decorated BiVO4 photoanode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Graduate Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant No. CYS18050 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis processes of various photoanodes using electrodeposition followed by annealing treatment and solvothermal method, Further, the SEM images of (b) BV, (c) NiO/BV, (d) CoO/BV, and (e) NiCoO2/BV, respectively. In addition, (f) and (g) are TEM and HRTEM image of NiCoO2/BV.
XPS of different samples, including (a) Bi 4f and (b) V 2p peaks for BV, (c) and (d) are the Ni 2p and Co 2p peaks for NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV, respectively.

The KPFM and surface potential results of (a) and (b) for BV, (c) and (d) for NiO/BV, (e) and (f) for CoO/BV, and (g) and (h) for NiCoO2/BV, the insert figures are the morphology of sample. The surface potential is collected by the different color's arrow. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

PEC performances of various photoanodes, (a) and (b) are the J-V curves for water and sulfite oxidation, while (c) and (d) are the efficiencies of charge separation and oxidation kinetics. The insert figure of (a) is the physical setup for PEC measurements.
The electrons' transport times (τ) at different light intensities (λ = 365 nm) for various photoanodes with water oxidation at 1.23 V vs. RHE.

(a) Illustration of the enhanced PEC water oxidation and (b) schematic diagram of the energy levels bending for cocatalysts decorated photoanode. CB and VB denote the conduction band and valence band of the BiVO4, which are collected by the Mott-Schottky and Tauc plots. For the band structure of OEC, the green, pink, and purple are representing the NiCoO2, CoO, and NiO, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

PEC performances of different photoanodes which including onset potentials for water oxidation (vs. RHE), photocurrents for water oxidation (at 1.23 V vs. RHE), charge separation efficiencies η sep (at 1.23 V vs. RHE), oxidation kinetics efficiencies η ox (at 1.23 V vs. RHE), and the transport time τ (at 300 W/m2), respectively.

head.ce:title: Guiding charge transfer kinetics into cocatalyst for efficient solar water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials, College of Physics, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National University of Singapore, Singapore, 117574, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials, College of Physics, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400030, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft Condensed Matter Physics and Smart Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of transition metal-based cocatalysts with semiconductors is a promising route to improve the photoelectrochemical performance in solar water splitting. However, it is difficult to evaluate the catalytic ability for cocatalysts quantitatively. Herein, we use kelvin probe force microscopy technique, combining with Mott-Schottky method, to investigate the cocatalysts from the point of surface potential and Femi level. Through synthesis the nickel/cobalt oxide nanoparticles and nanoporous bismuth vanadate (BiVO4), the result indicates the surface potential of NiCoO2 coated BiVO4 is lower than pristine BiVO4 and BiVO4 coated by NiO and CoO cocatalysts respectively. The lower surface potential means a more upward band bending structure at the interface of electrode and electrolyte, which can promote the separation of electron-hole pairs and enhance the charge transfer kinetics. Thus, the NiCoO2/BiVO4 photoanode achieve a remarkable photocurrent density of 2.34 mA/cm2 for water oxidation, and the charge separation and oxidation kinetics efficiencies are 61.9% and 72.7% respectively. In all, our studies provide a new mechanistic insight into cocatalysts for solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting based on semiconductors represents a green and low-cost method to generate hydrogen fuel [1–3]. The properties of semiconductor photoelectrodes are the primary concern in improving the overall efficiency of PEC cells, because they determine how much photoexcitation occurs in a semiconductor under illumination and how many photoexcited carriers reach the surface where water splitting takes place [4–6]. Recently, surface modification is ubiquitous for photoelectrodes since this approach may facilitate the charge separation and upgrade the stability of the semiconductors [7–9]. For instance, low-cost earth-abundant cocatalysts are loaded on the photoelectrodes to extract photogenerated carriers and host active sites for O2 evolution [10,11]. The cocatalysts play pivotal roles for semiconductors due to three factors: i) cocatalysts could lower the activation energy or overpotential for redox reaction on the surface of semiconductors; ii) cocatalysts can assist in electron-hole separation and transfer at the cocatalysts/semiconductors interface; and iii) cocatalysts could suppress the photocorrosion and increase the stability of photoelectrode [12,13].

Up to now, many cocatalysts were investigated (e.g. Fe(Mo, Ni)Px, Fe(Ni)OOH, and Ni(Co)Ox), especially for O2 evolution half reaction [14,15]. Kuang et al. discovered that the cobalt iron oxide evenly distributing on the surface of hematite nanorod arrays greatly facilitated the surface hole injection and promoted the charge separation [16], which led to an impressive 1.71-fold enhancement on the photocurrent density and about 170 mV negative shift of onset potential. This means that the cocatalyst decorated photoanode could enhance the charge transfer and decrease the onset potential [17]. Trudel et al. studied the α-Fe100-y-zCoyNizOx [18], revealed the catalytic properties of the multi-metal oxides were higher than α-Fe(Co, Ni)Ox and comparable to noble metal oxide. Based on our recognition, the surface potential of cocatalysts is critical for charge separation and transfer [19,20]. The lower surface potential means higher energy of Fermi level [21–23], which could change the charge distribution in space charge layer of materials and induce a built-in electric field. Li et al. found that MnOx cocatalyst could induce a built-in electric field beneath the uncovered surface of bismuth vanadate (BiVO4) [24], the additive electric fields caused a strong increase of local surface photovoltage signals (up to 80 times). However, very little research has addressed a method to evaluate an effective cocatalyst for solar water spitting quantitatively.

Generally, kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) is used to monitor the surface potential changes of materials, which allows for direct mapping the contact potential difference between the reference tip and the sample surface [25,26]. Hence, quantifying the surface potential and determining the position of Fermi level by KPFM are the most effective method. Tan et al. discovered that a contact potential shift of 0.22 eV was achieved in the Pt/n-Si system by KPFM evaluate [25]. Further, Yu et al. used KPFM to conduct the surface between two materials [26], and the electrified species (positive/negative) of the material surface could be confirmed by the location of Fermi level. The position of Fermi level is critical for charge transfer due to the electrons will naturally flow to lower energy state, while the holes are inverse. Therefore, if we can conduct the surface potential and band structure of a cocatalyst by an experimental method, screening a highly active cocatalyst for charge transfer will be very simple.

Herein, to study cocatalysts from the point of the band structure, we synthesized three kinds of oxygen evolution cocatalysts (OEC), NiO, CoO, and NiCoO2 nanoparticles (NPs), and decorated them on BiVO4 (BV) photoanodes. The surface potential of the composite photoelectrodes were investigated by KPFM. We found that the ability of charge separation and transfer of cocatalysts was related to their surface potential. Owing to the lower surface potential of NiCoO2 NPs decorated BiVO4, the photocurrent was enhanced by 175% compared with undecorated BiVO4 for water oxidation at 1.23 V vs. RHE. The fast charge transfer ability with OEC decorated photoanode could be ascribe to the upward band structure, and the NiCoO2 could hold more photogenerated holes for water oxidation than NiO and CoO. Here, we provide a detailed understanding of cocatalysts for PEC application, demonstrating a new route to explore solar light harvesting system by surface potential.

The BiVO4 was prepared through three steps. Firstly, the BiOI can be synthesized by electrodepositing. Briefly, 0.08 M Bi(NO3)3 solution was prepared by dissolving Bi(NO3)3 in 50 mL of a 0.8 M KI solution. The solution was mixed with 0.23 M p-benzoquinone in 20 mL absolute ethanol. It can be used for synthesis of BiOI nanoflakes due to the [BiI4]− species are formed by Bi3+ and I−. A typical three-electrode system was used to deposit BiOI nanoflakes, where an FTO substrate acted as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode acted as the reference electrode, and a platinum foil served as the counter electrode. To obtain the BiOI nanoflakes, −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl was applied on the working electrode for 200 s. Subsequently, the obtained BiOI electrodes were took out from the electrolyte and washed by absolute ethyl alcohol repeatedly, followed by drying at 60 °C for 12 h. Secondly, in order to completely convert BiOI into BiVO4, 0.15 g vanadyl acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2) was dissolved in 5 mL dimethyl sulfoxide as the source of vanadium, the obtained BiOI were soaked in 300 μL vanadium solution for 10 min and then annealed at 450 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C/min. Thirdly, excess V2O5 on the surface of BiVO4 were removed by soaking the samples in 1 M NaOH solution for 1.5 h under a gentle stirring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of OEC decorated BiVO4 photoanode
ce:section.ce:para: The nickel cobalt oxide NPs were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. Briefly, 1 mM Nickel(II) acetylacetonate (Ni(acac)2) and cobalt(II) acetylacetonate (Co(acac)2) were dispersed in 20 mL of tert-butanol under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 30 min, the BiVO4/FTO photoelectrode was transferred into an autoclave with 60° inclined angle and the sample is facing down. The autoclave was sealed and maintained at 200 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the electrodes were thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized water and dried at 60 °C with vacuum condition for later tests.

All photoanodes were prepared through electrodeposition, annealing, and solvothermal method [27], the schematic overview of the synthesis procedure is shown in Fig. 1 a. The BiOI nanoflake was used as the precursor for nanoporous BiVO4, demonstrating the flake is grown on FTO substrate vertically and the thickness is about 20 nm (Fig. S1). All OEC decorated samples were synthesized under the similar reaction conditions (solvothermal method 200 °C for 24 h) and the nomenclatures were displayed in Table S1. As shown in the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images (Fig. 1b), the BiVO4 was crystallized in a highly connected manner which have the nearly size (∼100 nm) and morphology. The SEM images clearly reveal that the novel nanoporous structure is constructed by numerous BiVO4 particles without obvious aggregation, which can ensure a strong light absorb ability and improve the utilization of photons [28,29]. Furthermore, the NiO, CoO, and NiCoO2 NPs exhibit a comparable size (∼10 nm) on the surface of BiVO4 uniformly (Fig. 1c–e). To confirm the quantity of these OECs, the atomic concentration was detected by inductively coupled plasma (ICP). These results suggest that NiO, CoO, and NiCoO2 have an equal metal atomic ratio (∼0.05 μmol/cm2) (Table S2), while the amount of Bi and V are comparable (∼0.73 μmol/cm2) for all samples. The result also demonstrates that the mass of OEC is about 3.74 μg/cm2, while the BiVO4 is nearly 236.46 μg/cm2, display that the mass loading percentage of cocatalyst is about 1.6%. Fig. 1f and g are the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-synthesized NiCoO2/BV, indicating the highly crystallized sample is obtained and the BiVO4 is surrounded by many NiCoO2 NPs. The inter layer distance (d-spacing) calculated from the lattice fringes pattern in Fig. 1g are 0.31 and 0.24 nm, which corresponds to the d-spacing value for (121) plane of BiVO4 and (111) plane of NiCoO2. In addition, the TEM images of NiO/BV and CoO/BV reveal that the exposed lattice plane of NiO and CoO is also the (111) plane compared with the NiCoO2, which are matched with the d-spacing values of 0.24 and 0.25 nm (Fig. S2) [29].

We further identified the bulk crystal structure of these semiconductors by X-Ray diffraction (XRD) technique. The XRD data of NiO, CoO, and NiCoO2 was obtained by powder samples (Fig. S3), indicating the synthesized OEC NPs are well crystalized. Further, the XRD patterns reveal that all the characteristic diffraction peaks are in good agreement with the standard data for monoclinic BiVO4 (04-1688) (Fig. S4a). It is worth mentioning that the positions of these peaks are only for FTO and BiVO4, no characteristic peaks of OEC NPs [28]. That is to say, the mass fraction of the decorated OECs is a small quantity compared with BiVO4 (1.6% confirmed by ICP tests). To examine the optical properties of these as-prepared samples, UV–vis absorption spectrum was performed and the results are shown in Fig. S4b. Obviously, the abrupt onset absorption of all photoanode is around 506 nm, this phenomenon could be attributed to the electronic transition from the valence band (Bi 6s) to conduction band (V 3d) of monoclinic BiVO4 upon excitation by photons [11]. When coupled with OEC NPs, the absorption of these samples was almost remaining unchanged, suggesting the OEC could not significantly affect the light absorption ability. Moreover, the result in Tauc plots sharing a similar absorption property and indicate the bandgap of these semiconductors is nearly 2.45 eV (insert of Fig. S4b), which corresponding with other researches [30,31].

Next, we used X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to characterize the surface composition of these as-prepared samples. XPS survey scans for all samples are shown in Fig. S5. The peaks of C and Sn elements are attributed to carbon from air and SnO2 from the FTO substrate, while the C 1s peak was calibrated to 284.8 eV. After Shirley background subtraction for the individual element core level, the Bi 4f core level peaks Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 fit well to single-component with binding energies of 159.1 and 164.4 eV (Δ = 5.3 eV) (Fig. 2 a), revealing unambiguous assignment to Bi3+ [32]. The V 2p core level peaks V 2p3/2 and V 2p1/2 have binding energies of 516.9 and 524.4 eV (Δ = 7.5 eV) (Fig. 2b), suggesting to V5+ in a typical BiVO4. In addition, there is no evidence for V4+, which is expected to have a binding energy of 515.5–516.0 eV [33]. Furthermore, the high-resolution spectrum of Ni 2p of NiO and NiCoO2 exhibit two peaks located at 855.5 and 873.5 eV (Fig. 2c), indicating the electronic states of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 [28,34]. Each main peak is accompanied by a plump shake-up satellite, which suggests the electronic state of Ni2+. There is no evidence for Ni3+, which is expected to have a higher binding energy at Ni 2p3/2 (∼856.4 eV). The Co spectrum of CoO and NiCoO2 in Fig. 2d shows two peaks at the binding energies of 780.4 and 796.4 eV, which could be attributed to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively [35,36]. The shake-up satellite peaks accompanying the Co 2p peak indicate the existence of Co2+, and there is no evidence for Co3+, which is expected to have a lower binding energy at Co 2p3/2 (∼779.6 eV).

To reveal the work function and Fermi level of these photoanodes, KPFM was used to test the surface potential (Vsurface) [37–39]. Fig. 3 shows the topology and Vsurface of these as-prepared samples, indicating the comparable morphology of the as-prepared samples. As the KPFM image provides the contact potential difference between the tip and the sample, the darker color scale indicates that this area has a more negative Vsurface. In contrast, the brighter images suggest this area has a more positive Vsurface. Surprisingly, the potential diversity with various samples shows that the cocatalysts have a significantly effect on surface structure. According to the results, the surface potential gap between FTO substrate and pristine BV, NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are 42.5, 31.8, 24.6, and 17.4 mV, respectively. These results indicate that the Vsurface of NiCoO2/BV decreased 25.1 mV compared with pristine BV, and the NiCoO2 has a lower Vsurface than that of NiO and CoO. The changes in Vsurface clearly reflect the charge balance on the surface of photoanode, the decreased Vsurface suggests a lower contact potential of the photoelectrode. Other researchers demonstrated that [26], for a semiconductor, the measured Vsurface was related to the work function as the following equation:
(1)
Φ
s a m p l e
=
Φ
r e f e r e n c e
− e ·
V
s u r f a c e
where the Φsample and Φreference are the work function of materials and the probe. So, we can obtain the Fermi level (EF) of a semiconductor according to the relationship of work function and vacuum energy level of electron (W),
(2)
Φ
s a m p l e
= W −
E
F

The lower work function means the higher Fermi level, which will induce the charge redistribution in space charge layer and generate a built-in electric field [40]. This result implies that the Fermi level is up shifted 0.0251 eV for NiCoO2/BV compared with BV. The oriented electric field could enhance the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer holes to the surface of photoanode for oxidation reaction [24].

In many n-type semiconductors, the conduction band edge is often considered to be more negative by about 0.1–0.2 eV than its flatband potential [3]. So, we could investigate the flatband to elucidate the band structure of the as-prepared samples. To confirm the flatband potentials of these photoanodes, Mott-Schottky plots at different frequencies were conducted in 0.5 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0). The result shows that the slope of log(-Z'') vs. log(f) is −1 at a broad frequency region (500-5000 Hz) (Fig. S6a), demonstrating the 1000 and 3000 Hz is reasonable for Mott-Schottky measurement. These plots present a quasilinear behavior (Fig. S6b–d), revealing the flatband potentials of the NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are around 0.12, 0.11, and 0.04 V vs. RHE, respectively. The negative shift of flatband potential for NiCoO2/BV suggests the NiCoO2 has a higher Fermi level compared with NiO and CoO cocatalysts, displaying the enhanced hole transfer kinetics from BiVO4 to NiCoO2. Further, the slops of these Mott-Schottky plots reflect the concentration of carriers with photoanodes under illumination [3]. These results confirmed that the NiCoO2 decorated photoanode has the smaller slop (larger carrier concentration) than NiO and CoO, exhibiting the promoted charge separation efficiency for NiCoO2/BV structure.

To gain a better understanding how these cocatalysts affect the activity of photoanodes in PEC reaction, typical current-potential (J-V) curves of all samples were acquired in 0.5 M phosphate buffer solution under AM 1.5G illumination (standard simulated sunlight), the real react area is fixed to 1 cm2 by a block (inset of Fig. 4 a). The photocurrent responses of all photoanodes with/without illumination results could reveal the ability of charge separation and oxidation kinetics. Clearly, the unmodified BiVO4 demonstrate a relatively low photocurrent at 1.23 V vs. RHE (0.85 mA/cm2) compared with cocatalyst decorated photoanode (Fig. 4a). This phenomenon is ascribed to the poor charge transfer ability with water oxidation for unmodified photoanode, and the photogenerated electron-holes are easy to recombine at the surface of photoanode. The NiCoO2/BV photoanode displayed an enhanced photocurrent of 2.34 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and the enhancement of photocurrent is 175% compared with pristine BiVO4. This significantly improved photocurrent is also much higher than NiO/BV and CoO/BV (1.42 and 1.49 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE) due to a lower Vsurface. Compared with other researches, our results displayed a greatly enhancement for photocurrent with OECs coated BiVO4 photoanodes (Table S3). Furthermore, the onset potential of BV, NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are 0.50, 0.45, 0.22, and 0.21 V vs. RHE, respectively, indicating the NiCoO2 cocatalysts can effectively reduce the barrier of carriers’ transfer [27]. Here, the negative shift of onset potential result is also corresponding well with the negative shift of flatband potential. However, the charge separation and transfer rate of these photoanodes need to be further studied by quantitative analysis. Adding SO3 2− to the electrolyte can completely suppress the surface recombination of carriers since the SO3 2− can absorb the photogenerated holes to generate the SO4 2− quickly [12]. Then, the value of sulfite oxidation photocurrent can reflect the number of electrons which are generated by the absorbed light. Hence, we measured these photoanodes with J-V curves in 0.5 M phosphate buffer solution containing 0.2 M Na2SO3 (Fig. 4b). The sulfite oxidation photocurrent density of BV, NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are 1.90, 2.42, 2.50, and 3.22 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, respectively, suggesting the efficiencies of charge separation are diversity and the cocatalysts have a significant impact for charge separation. All in all, the PEC activities of these photoanodes could be ranked with the following order: BV < NiO/BV < CoO/BV < NiCoO2/BV. Additionally, the efficiency of charge separation (η sep) and surface charge transfer (η ox, relating to surface charge oxidation kinetics) could be determined independently by η sep ≈ J sulfite/J abs and η ox ≈ J water/J sulfite (details in supporting information) [12,17], which displayed in Fig. 4c and d. These results reveal that η sep for NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are 46.5%, 48.1%, and 61.9% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, demonstrating the cocatalyst with lower Vsurface could promote the charge separation. Moreover, η ox is improved from 43.6% for BV to 72.7% for NiCoO2/BV at 1.23 V vs. RHE (NiO/BV and CoO/BV are 57.6% and 59.6%), showing a great advantage for charge transfer ascribe to the lower Vsurface of cocatalysts. These results elucidate that the NiCoO2 decorated BiVO4 photoanode is beneficial for charge separation and transfer, which could be explained that the lower Vsurface could induce a higher Fermi level to guide charge migration [25].

To shed light on the charge transfer of photogenerated electrons from the surface of the photoanodes to the FTO substrate, we plotted the value of electrons transport time (τ) with different power density at 1.23 V vs. RHE for various photoanodes (the excite wavelength is 365 nm) in 0.5 M phosphate buffer. The photogenerated electron's transport time could be determined by the controlled intensity modulated photocurrent spectrometer (CIMPS) measurement according to the imaginary components (Fig. S7) [41]. As for the CIMPS results, Fig. 5 plots the charge transport time (τ) as the function of the power density for all samples. Obviously, the photogenerated electrons in BV photoanode have a longer transport time, suggesting lower charge collection efficiency. Specifically, the τ values at the maximum light intensity (300 W/m2) for NiO/BV, CoO/BV, and NiCoO2/BV are 0.30, 0.29, and 0.26 ms during water oxidation. The distinct differences of these transport time are closely contacted with the efficiency of oxidation kinetics, demonstrating the lower Vsurface cocatalysts (multi-metal oxide) can remarkably enhance the carriers' mobility. In addition, these results are in accordance with their J-V curves for water oxidation and suggest faster electrons transfer rate for NiCoO2/BV.

To further understand the mechanism of the cocatalysts from the point of band structure, Fig. 6 a shows the photoelectrocatalyst process on OEC decorated photoanodes. When solar light radiates on the photoanode, the photogenerated electrons and holes are induced immediately in BiVO4. The cocatalysts served as the hole injection layer and promote the separation of electron-hole pairs [42]. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons transport to the FTO substrate under the extra positive bias. At the same time, due to the strong oxidation kinetics of holes, the Ni2+/Co2+ ions could be oxidized to a higher state (Ni4+/Co4+). Then, the Ni4+/Co4+ ions are reduced to Ni2+/Co2+ by withdrawing four electrons from the water (produce oxygen). In brief, the process of reaction can be written as the following five steps:
(3)
h v
→
BiVO
4
h
+
+
e
−
(4)
2
e
−
+ 2
H
+
→
2
H
2
↑
(5)
Ni
2 +
/
Co
2 +
+ 2
h
+
→
Ni
3 +
/
Co
3 +
(6)
Ni
3 +
/
Co
3 +
+ 2
h
+
→
Ni
4 +
/
Co
4 +
(7)
Ni
4 +
/
Co
4 +
+ 2
H
2
O
→
Ni
2 +
/
Co
2 +
+ 4
H
+
+
O
2
↑

The corresponding energy level of BiVO4 after deposited by OEC NPs is shown in Fig. 6b. By coating a lower Vsurface cocatalyst on photoanode, more photogenerated holes could transport to the photoanode/electrolyte interface due to the lower work function, and the more upward bending of band structure would promote the transport of the photogenerated electrons from photoanode to the FTO substrate [24]. Thus, the electron-hole pairs are difficult to recombine and the water oxidation kinetics on the surface of photoanode is reinforced. The OEC could control the transfer speed of the carriers by adjusting the band bending state, and cocatalyst with the lower Vsurface has a higher charge separation and transfer ability. Specifically, we can select the most suitable cocatalyst to decorate photoanode according to the Vsurface, which clearly gives us a sign that OEC could change the energy band structure of semiconductors [28].

In summary, different cocatalysts modified BiVO4 photoanodes were designed and prepared. KPFM measurements indicate that the Vsurface of NiCoO2/BV decreased 25.1 mV compared with pristine BV. The lower Vsurface means a higher Fermi level, indicating the upward band bending at the interface between BiVO4 and cocatalyst. This band structure could promote the separation of electron-hole pairs and guide the transport of carriers. Thus, the NiCoO2/BV photoanode achieved a remarkable photocurrent density of 2.34 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE with water oxidation (pH 7.0), which is about 2.75 times higher than the pristine BV (0.85 mA/cm2). In addition, the charge separation efficiency and the oxidation kinetics efficiency enhanced to 61.9% and 72.7% for NiCoO2/BV at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is higher than other composite structure. Our results suggest an effective method to evaluate an energetic cocatalyst according to the value of Vsurface, and provide a vital information on the design and development of the next-generation PEC electrode structure.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Graduate Research and Innovation Foundation of Chongqing, China (Grant No. CYS18050).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.03.182.
